and you want to run it many, many times or continually feed it more and more data, and you want it to get better and better over time, that is basically Letta. where they're kind of outlining what is an agent. So a lot of the stuff I'm saying about learning being extremely important and memory being kind of, like, a cornerstone of human-like intelligence, this is not new stuff, right? I think if you go and pick up this really old book on reinforcement learning by Sun Lombardo, it's kind of, like, the RL canon, and you scroll to, like, the first few pages,

it's very, very quickly you start to see the word learn. And this is the kind of diagram you see to describe what an agent is. This is, like, prior to ChatGPT. This is, like, the agent in AI before that. You know, I think that there's very few elements here. It's, like, you have the agent, you have the environment,

you have the reward, the observations, the actions. I think everything here we already have, right? The agent is the language model. The action is the tools, the MCP servers, whatever. or it's running inside of, like, your internal business. And I think the one thing that's missing that doesn't really exist in most systems today is, like, the state. And I think the key part that makes this a very important part of this diagram is it's a loop. It's a circle, right?

And the reason it's a closed loop is because the agent updates its own state or updates its own memory as it continues to act in this environment. And, yeah, this is where I think maybe things get a little bit more interesting, is that this learning, I don't think you have to do this learning only at inference time,

right, when you kind of interact with the agent. I think right now what happens when you use ChatGPT is ChatGPT, like, when you're using it, it's interacting with you. It's, like, you know, there's, the brain is cooking on some GPU farm somewhere. But when you leave ChatGPT, ChatGPT is, like, completely dormant

and never does anything until you come back, right? I think there's a lot of, like, missed opportunity to be doing things in the background, right? You can imagine, like, Claude code. there are two main directions to scale compute. If you assume that model performance is, like, saturated or is, like, saturating to some point or has diminishing returns, right? I think the obvious one that we've heard about for the past year is test-time compute. You, like, let the language models

run for longer. They get better and better answers because they get to think for longer. And the other one is, well, you don't just run them for longer and longer when the user asks. You run them for longer and longer all the time, right? As much as you can, as much as it kind of, like, makes meaningful sense.

And I think, yeah, that's kind of the direction everything is going in. language models themselves are not stateful agents. Well, what do we have to build? Like, what's left? If we have the models, the best models on the planet Earth given to us by these frontier labs, what's left to make these sorts of stateful agents? And I think really the best way

to frame what's left is it's effectively building an operating system. But it's building an operating system whose primary function or primary objective is to orchestrate context. So there was a talk recently by Andrei Karpathy where he talked about context engineering, right?

And I think actually the tweet or something was yesterday or today. So this terminal is kind of taking off. So I think that's exactly what I'm talking about here as well. It's basically you need some sort of system that is going to do context engineering on your behalf to move tokens

in and out of the context window. But beyond that, I think that's it. You basically, I think the map or the road to AGI is very simple. It's you have the language models and you need to build the operating system to manage the context. And I think those are the two parts. And I think a lot of us

probably have heard about the LMOS operating system thing. That's also something that we were talking about and also Andrei was talking about as far back as like 2023. So yeah, the name of the paper MemGPT was actually MemGPT towards language models as operating systems. So an operating system

for language models driven by language models. So yeah, this idea, we've been working on it for a while. And yeah, we really think this is kind of the last missing piece. So one way to describe it is it's basically the open AI operating system. So all the stuff

we're describing today, this dream of having the truly stateful agent, well, it's definitely not just my dream. I think it's also the dream of Sam Altman and other people running these frontier labs. But I think Leta is the only company that's really building this sort of software in the open as open source.

And I think one interesting thing that I'm not sure if it's on these slides, but an interesting thought experiment you can do is if we believe we are actually moving into a world where we have these agents that are no longer workflows, they last for days, weeks, months, years.

Just think about how often you swap out your models, right? How often does the best model whiplash from open AI to anthropic to deep seek, right? I think we're very quickly moving to a world where the lifetime of the agents are much longer than the lifetime

of the models. for example, and have them locked into a single model provider, right? of the equation, not just the model, but for the system around it for that to be open. I think that there's just too many incentives for that to be closed. if you have agents that run for like, you know, five, ten years, there's no way that the most important part of that agent, if the agent is just state, memory, and the model, is the model, right? It's the state and the memory. And yeah, functionally, how you would actually

so easy to use also makes these agent libraries so easy to throw away. It makes it so easy for you to swap from OpenAI Agents SDK to, you know, LangChain to, like, whatever agent library because really these are just, it's like middleware that's helping you use the model providers,

but it's not necessarily, it's not dealing with this thing on the left which is like the state and the memory context that's storage.
